events.text: The patient is a 63-year-old female named Margaret Okonkwo. She has a history of hypertension managed with amlodipine, hyperlipidemia on a statin, and a remote 20-pack-year smoking history (quit at age 48). She is of West African descent, a retired secondary school administrator, and lives with her adult daughter. Performance status ECOG 1 at initial presentation.

At age 63 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition; cT3bN2M1) RENAL CELL CARCINOMA, CLEAR CELL TYPE (ICD-O-3 histology code 8310/3), primary site RIGHT KIDNEY (ICD-O-3 site code C64.9). The right renal mass measured 11.4 cm with extension into the right renal vein and infrarenal inferior vena cava (cT3b). Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy was present with multiple nodes up to 2.8 cm in the aortocaval region (N2). Bilateral pulmonary metastases were identified, the largest measuring 1.9 cm in the right lower lobe. Sarcomatoid differentiation was present in approximately 20% of the sampled tumor. Summary stage: DISTANT. IMDC risk group: Intermediate (2 factors: Karnofsky <80 at nadir during workup, hemoglobin below lower limit of normal). Relevant biomarkers: PD-L1 combined positive score (CPS) estimated high by biopsy specimen; no rhabdoid differentiation noted. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY. Tumor size: 114 mm. Laterality: RIGHT.

At age 63 years, the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (staging scan) which showed cancer. The right kidney contained an 11.4 cm heterogeneous mass with areas of central necrosis, involvement of the right renal vein and infrarenal IVC (approximately 3 cm of IVC thrombus, non-occlusive). Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy was identified: right para-aortic nodes up to 2.8 cm, aortocaval node 2.2 cm, and right common iliac chain node 1.5 cm. Pulmonary findings: bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with metastatic disease, right lower lobe 1.9 cm, right upper lobe 1.1 cm, left lower lobe 0.8 cm, left upper lobe 0.6 cm. No hepatic, osseous, or adrenal metastases identified. No brain metastases on dedicated MRI brain (performed separately). Metastatic sites: PULMONARY, LYMPH NODES (RETROPERITONEAL).

At age 63 years, the patient had an MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST (staging evaluation) which showed no cancer. No intracranial metastases, leptomeningeal disease, or other intracranial pathology identified. Incidental finding of mild age-appropriate cerebral white matter changes.

At age 63 years, the patient had a pathology result from a CT-GUIDED CORE NEEDLE BIOPSY of the right renal mass (pre-operative confirmation). Histology: RENAL CELL CARCINOMA, CLEAR CELL TYPE (ccRCC). The tumor cells demonstrated clear cytoplasm, prominent vascularity, and Fuhrman/WHO-ISUP nuclear grade 3. Sarcomatoid differentiation was identified, comprising approximately 20% of the sampled specimen, with spindle cell morphology and increased mitotic activity in the sarcomatoid component. No rhabdoid differentiation. Immunohistochemistry: PAX8 positive, CA9 (carbonic anhydrase IX) positive (diffuse membranous), vimentin positive in sarcomatoid component, CK7 focally positive, CD10 positive. PD-L1 IHC (22C3 antibody): TPS 40%, CPS estimated 55 (high). Ki-67 proliferation index approximately 35% in sarcomatoid areas, 18% in conventional clear cell areas. PBRM1 expression: loss by IHC (suggestive of somatic mutation). BAP1: retained expression. Margins not applicable (needle biopsy).

At age 63 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for clear cell renal cell carcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the right renal mass core biopsy. Platform: comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP), 324-gene panel. Findings: VHL p.R161* (nonsense mutation, pathogenic, allele frequency 48%, known driver in ccRCC), PBRM1 c.2977+1G>T (splice site mutation, pathogenic, allele frequency 42%, loss of function), SETD2 p.L1916Rfs*15 (frameshift, pathogenic, allele frequency 31%, SWI/SNF complex member), KDM5C p.T724M (missense, variant of uncertain significance, allele frequency 18%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion (copy number loss, high-level), chromosome 3p loss (consistent with VHL locus loss of heterozygosity), chromosome 5q gain. No KRAS, NRAS, BRAF mutations detected. No MET amplification. No FGFR fusions or alterations. No mismatch repair deficiency (pMMR); microsatellite stable (MSS). Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 6.2 mutations/megabase (intermediate-low). Genomic LOH: 18% (intermediate). No actionable drug-matched alterations beyond standard-of-care VEGFR/mTOR/immune checkpoint therapy. PD-L1 expression confirmed high by CGP-associated IHC. Germline analysis: no pathogenic germline variants in VHL, SDHA/B/C/D, FH, TSC1/2, MET, or FLCN detected.

At age 63 years, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board (MDT) assessment. The case was presented to the genitourinary oncology MDT. Discussion: The patient has IMDC intermediate-risk metastatic ccRCC with sarcomatoid features and high PD-L1 expression. The retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy and IVC thrombus are technically resectable. The pulmonary metastases are bilateral and numerous, precluding definitive local therapy to all metastatic sites. The MDT recommended: (1) cytoreductive nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection and IVC thrombectomy given good performance status and bulk of disease in the primary, (2) first-line systemic therapy with ipilimumab/nivolumab post-operatively given sarcomatoid features and high PD-L1 (per CheckMate 214 and sarcomatoid subgroup data). Cancer present. Disease: progressing (newly diagnosed metastatic).

At age 63 years, the patient underwent CYTOREDUCTIVE RIGHT RADICAL NEPHRECTOMY with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND) and inferior vena cava thrombectomy. Operative approach: open (midline laparotomy). Findings: 11.8 cm right renal mass (slightly larger than CT estimate), IVC thrombus extending 4 cm into the infrarenal IVC (level I/II), multiple matted retroperitoneal lymph nodes. Complete resection of renal primary with negative surgical margins. IVC thrombus excised; IVC wall was uninvolved. RPLND: 14 lymph nodes harvested. Estimated blood loss: 850 mL; 2 units packed red blood cells transfused intraoperatively. No intraoperative complications. Post-operative course: admitted for 5 days. Developed mild ileus on post-operative day 2, resolved with conservative management. Discharged in good condition.

At age 63 years (post-nephrectomy), the patient had a surgical pathology result from the RIGHT RADICAL NEPHRECTOMY specimen. Final diagnosis: RENAL CELL CARCINOMA, CLEAR CELL TYPE, pT3bN2. Tumor size: 11.8 cm greatest dimension. Fuhrman/WHO-ISUP grade: 4 (sarcomatoid differentiation present, 20-25% of tumor volume). Sarcomatoid differentiation: present, estimated 22% of tumor. Rhabdoid differentiation: absent. Tumor necrosis: present (extensive, >50%). Lymphovascular invasion: present. Renal vein: involved by direct extension. IVC thrombus: Level I-II, 4.2 cm, non-occlusive; IVC wall margins negative. Adrenal gland: uninvolved, not resected with specimen. Surgical margins: negative (closest margin 2 mm, renal sinus fat). Lymph nodes: 9 of 14 lymph nodes positive for metastatic ccRCC (9/14); largest involved node 3.1 cm with extranodal extension. Sarcomatoid component also present in lymph node metastases.

At age 63 years (approximately 6 weeks post-nephrectomy, pre-systemic therapy baseline scan), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST. Cancer present. Metastatic disease identified: pulmonary metastases stable to slightly increased compared to staging CT: right lower lobe 2.1 cm (previously 1.9 cm), right upper lobe 1.2 cm (previously 1.1 cm), left lower lobe 0.9 cm (previously 0.8 cm), left upper lobe 0.7 cm (previously 0.6 cm). Post-surgical changes in the right retroperitoneum. No new sites of disease. No hepatic or osseous metastases. This scan establishes the new baseline for systemic therapy response assessment. Metastatic sites: PULMONARY (bilateral, 4 lesions).

At age 63 years (approximately 8 weeks post-surgery), the patient initiated FIRST-LINE systemic therapy with IPILIMUMAB 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (for 4 doses) plus NIVOLUMAB 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (for 4 induction doses), followed by NIVOLUMAB 480 mg IV every 4 weeks (maintenance), per CheckMate 214 protocol. Treatment intent: palliative (disease control/overall survival benefit in metastatic setting). Rationale: IMDC intermediate risk, sarcomatoid features (22%), high PD-L1 (CPS 55), prior nephrectomy completed. Premedication: acetaminophen and diphenhydramine per institutional protocol. Baseline labs: CBC within normal limits (Hgb 10.8 g/dL, mildly low, attributed to post-surgical state), CMP within normal limits, TSH 1.8 mIU/L (normal), cortisol AM 14.2 mcg/dL (normal), LFTs within normal limits.

At age 63 years (cycle 2, week 6 of ipilimumab/nivolumab), the patient had an oncologist office visit. The patient tolerated cycle 1 well. Cycle 2 administered. She reports mild fatigue (grade 1), mild nausea (grade 1, managed with ondansetron PRN), and two loose stools per day above baseline (grade 1 diarrhea). No rash, no arthralgia. ECOG PS: 1. Labs: TSH 3.2 mIU/L (normal), LFTs within normal limits, CBC with mild anemia (Hgb 10.4 g/dL). Cancer present (pre-response assessment). No immune-related adverse events requiring intervention at this time. Continue therapy as planned.

At age 63 years (cycle 3, approximately week 10 of ipilimumab/nivolumab), the patient developed IMMUNE-RELATED COLITIS (irAE), CTCAE Grade 2. She presented with 6-8 loose/watery stools per day above baseline, associated with mild lower abdominal cramping. No blood in stool. Colonoscopy performed: diffuse mild colonic mucosal edema and erythema, consistent with immune-mediated colitis; biopsies showed increased intraepithelial lymphocytes and crypt distortion. Management: ipilimumab held (nivolumab continued with caution); oral budesonide 9 mg daily initiated. Stool cultures negative. Symptoms improved to grade 1 within 10 days. Ipilimumab re-introduced at next cycle after resolution to grade 1 or less.

At age 63 years (approximately 12 weeks/3 months of ipilimumab/nivolumab, first restaging), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST. Cancer present. Response assessment per RECIST 1.1: PARTIAL RESPONSE. Pulmonary metastases decreased: right lower lobe 1.3 cm (from 2.1 cm, -38%), right upper lobe 0.7 cm (from 1.2 cm, -42%), left lower lobe 0.5 cm (from 0.9 cm, -44%), left upper lobe 0.4 cm (from 0.7 cm, -43%). No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No new metastatic sites. Sum of diameters: 2.9 cm (from baseline 5.0 cm, -42%). This confirms partial response. Metastatic sites: PULMONARY (bilateral, 4 lesions, all decreased).

At age 63 years (after completing 4 cycles of ipilimumab induction, transitioning to nivolumab maintenance), the patient had an oncologist office visit. Completed 4 doses of ipilimumab (1 dose was held due to grade 2 colitis, completed the 4th dose with 1-cycle delay). Now transitioning to nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks (maintenance). Restaging CT shows partial response (-42%). Patient feeling better overall, fatigue improved (grade 1). Colitis now grade 1 on budesonide taper (being weaned). TSH: 0.4 mIU/L (mildly low, monitoring). Adrenal function normal. Cancer present with response to therapy. Continue nivolumab maintenance.

At age 63 years (approximately 5 months into therapy, during nivolumab maintenance), the patient developed IMMUNE-RELATED HYPOTHYROIDISM (irAE), CTCAE Grade 2. She reported increasing fatigue, cold intolerance, and mild constipation over several weeks. TSH: 18.4 mIU/L (markedly elevated), free T4: 0.6 ng/dL (low). Thyroid peroxidase antibodies: positive (high titer). Diagnosis: immune checkpoint inhibitor-associated hypothyroidism. Nivolumab continued without interruption. Levothyroxine initiated at 50 mcg daily, titrated to 75 mcg daily over subsequent weeks with normalization of TSH (follow-up TSH: 2.1 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg). Symptoms improved substantially. Levothyroxine to be continued indefinitely.

At age 63 years (approximately 6 months of systemic therapy, second restaging), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST. Cancer present. Response assessment per RECIST 1.1: MIXED RESPONSE. Pulmonary metastases: right lower lobe lesion increased from 1.3 cm to 2.4 cm (+85% from nadir, NEW target lesion progression); right upper lobe 0.6 cm (stable from 0.7 cm); left lower lobe 0.5 cm (stable); left upper lobe 0.4 cm (stable). NEW finding: a 1.3 cm right adrenal nodule, new since baseline, consistent with adrenal metastasis (biopsy recommended vs. follow-up). Sum of diameters of target lesions: 3.9 cm (from nadir 2.9 cm, +34%). Overall assessment: mixed response / early oligoprogression at right lower lobe lesion and possible new adrenal metastasis. Metastatic sites: PULMONARY, ADRENAL (new, right).

At age 63 years (approximately 6.5 months of systemic therapy), the patient had an MDT reassessment for mixed response on ipilimumab/nivolumab. Discussion: Patient has mixed response - most pulmonary lesions stable but right lower lobe lesion has significantly increased and a new right adrenal metastasis has appeared. This represents oligoprogression at 2 sites. Options discussed: (1) Continue nivolumab with local therapy to the right lower lobe (SBRT) and adrenal (SBRT or adrenalectomy); (2) Discontinue immunotherapy and switch to cabozantinib; (3) Continue and re-image at 8 weeks. Decision: given that only 2 sites are progressing, proceed with SBRT to the right lower lobe pulmonary lesion. Right adrenal biopsy planned to confirm metastatic disease before further intervention. Continue nivolumab for now while local therapy is arranged. Cancer present, mixed response to immunotherapy.

At age 63 years (approximately 7 months into therapy), the patient had a CT-guided core biopsy of the RIGHT ADRENAL NODULE. Histology: METASTATIC RENAL CELL CARCINOMA, CLEAR CELL TYPE, consistent with the known primary. Tumor cells demonstrate clear cytoplasm, PAX8 positive, CA9 positive, CD10 positive. Sarcomatoid features not identified in this specimen. Ki-67: 22%. This confirms adrenal metastasis from known ccRCC primary.

At age 63 years (approximately 7.5 months into therapy), the patient underwent STEREOTACTIC BODY RADIATION THERAPY (SBRT) to the RIGHT LOWER LOBE PULMONARY METASTASIS (2.4 cm). Technique: SBRT, linac-based, 4D-CT simulation with respiratory gating. Dose: 54 Gy in 3 fractions (18 Gy per fraction), delivered over 1 week. Planning target volume: 2.4 cm lesion with 0.5 cm isotropic margin. Treatment well-tolerated. No acute toxicity beyond mild fatigue. Concurrent nivolumab held for the SBRT week and resumed the following week. Radiation oncology follow-up arranged.

At age 63 years (approximately 9 months of systemic therapy, third restaging), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST and MRI BRAIN WITH CONTRAST. Cancer present. Overall assessment: PROGRESSIVE DISEASE per RECIST 1.1. Findings: RIGHT LOWER LOBE PULMONARY LESION: treated with SBRT, now 1.8 cm (radiation response, local control achieved). Right upper lobe lesion: increased from 0.6 cm to 1.8 cm (+200%). Left lower lobe lesion: increased from 0.5 cm to 1.4 cm (+180%). Left upper lobe lesion: increased from 0.4 cm to 0.9 cm (+125%). NEW hepatic lesion: segment VI, 1.6 cm, new since prior imaging. RIGHT ADRENAL nodule: 1.5 cm, slight increase from 1.3 cm (+15%). Sum of diameters: 7.4 cm (from baseline 5.0 cm, +48% from baseline; +155% from nadir). MRI brain: no intracranial metastases. Assessment: systemic progressive disease on nivolumab maintenance despite local SBRT to RLL lesion. Metastatic sites: PULMONARY (bilateral, multiple), HEPATIC (new), ADRENAL (right).

At age 63 years (9 months after starting ipilimumab/nivolumab), the patient had an oncologist office visit for progression assessment. Restaging imaging confirms progressive disease: multiple pulmonary lesions increased, new hepatic metastasis, adrenal progression. ECOG PS: 1. Labs: Hgb 9.8 g/dL (mild anemia), LFTs mildly elevated (ALT 48, AST 52, likely hepatic metastasis), creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (normal), eGFR 58 mL/min (normal for single kidney post-nephrectomy). Alkaline phosphatase: 124 U/L (mildly elevated). Decision: DISCONTINUE nivolumab. Initiate second-line therapy with CABOZANTINIB (VEGFR/MET/AXL inhibitor). Hepatic lesion to be monitored; not amenable to SBRT given multiple progressing systemic sites. SBRT to adrenal considered but deferred given systemic progression. Cancer present, progressing on immunotherapy.

At age 63 years (approximately 9.5 months after initial systemic therapy start), the patient initiated SECOND-LINE systemic therapy with CABOZANTINIB 60 mg orally once daily (starting dose per institutional protocol; label dose is 60 mg/day). Treatment intent: palliative (disease control). Rationale: progression on first-line ipilimumab/nivolumab; cabozantinib is standard second-line therapy after immunotherapy in ccRCC with favorable Phase 3 data (METEOR trial, CABOSUN subgroup data). Baseline creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL. Patient counseled on expected toxicities: hypertension, hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia), diarrhea, fatigue, nausea, hepatotoxicity, and hypothyroidism (already on levothyroxine). Blood pressure at baseline: 138/84 mmHg (slightly elevated; amlodipine 10 mg/day already in use, dose not changed yet). Baseline TSH: 2.1 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg.

At age 63 years (approximately 3 weeks into cabozantinib therapy), the patient developed PALMAR-PLANTAR ERYTHRODYSESTHESIA (hand-foot syndrome, HFS), CTCAE Grade 2. She presented with painful erythema, swelling, and blistering on bilateral palms and soles, interfering with daily activities. She also reported 5-6 loose stools per day (Grade 2 diarrhea), with associated mild cramping but no blood. Management: cabozantinib dose reduced from 60 mg to 40 mg daily per protocol. Urea cream (40%) applied to affected areas twice daily; blister dressings for plantar lesions. Loperamide 4 mg initial dose then 2 mg after each loose stool for diarrhea management. Patient educated on foot care: soft shoes, padded insoles, avoid heat/friction. Dermatology consultation placed. Symptoms improved to grade 1 over the subsequent 2-3 weeks.

At age 63 years (approximately 6 weeks into cabozantinib 40 mg/day), the patient developed HYPERTENSION, CTCAE Grade 2. Blood pressure: 158/96 mmHg on repeated measurements. Amlodipine dose adjusted from 10 mg to maximum; lisinopril 10 mg daily added (also nephroprotective in setting of single kidney). Goal BP <140/90 mmHg. Subsequent BP: 138/88 mmHg (improved, within goal). Cabozantinib dose maintained at 40 mg. Electrolytes monitored (potassium 4.1 mEq/L, normal). Urinalysis: trace proteinuria (1+), 24-hour urine protein pending (ultimately 680 mg/24h, below threshold for dose modification).

At age 63 years (approximately 3 months of cabozantinib therapy, first restaging on new regimen), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST. Cancer present. Response assessment per RECIST 1.1: STABLE DISEASE (SD). Pulmonary metastases: right upper lobe 1.5 cm (from 1.8 cm, -17%); left lower lobe 1.2 cm (from 1.4 cm, -14%); left upper lobe 0.8 cm (from 0.9 cm, -11%). Right lower lobe: previously irradiated lesion 1.4 cm, stable/slight decrease. Hepatic segment VI lesion: 1.3 cm (from 1.6 cm, -19%). Right adrenal: 1.3 cm (stable from 1.5 cm, -13%). Sum of target lesion diameters: decreased from 7.4 cm (at cabozantinib initiation) to 6.2 cm (-16%), meeting criteria for stable disease (not sufficient for PR). No new metastatic sites. MRI brain not repeated (no neurologic symptoms). Assessment: stable disease on cabozantinib with minor tumor regression across all sites. Metastatic sites: PULMONARY (bilateral), HEPATIC (segment VI), ADRENAL (right).

At age 63 years (approximately 3 months after starting cabozantinib), the patient had an oncologist office visit for response assessment. Restaging CT shows stable disease with minor decreases across all metastatic sites. Patient reports improved quality of life compared to initial progression period. Current toxicities: hand-foot syndrome grade 1 (managed with urea cream and protective footwear); diarrhea 2-3 stools above baseline (grade 1, managed with dietary modification and PRN loperamide); fatigue grade 1-2. ECOG PS: 1. Labs: TSH 8.2 mIU/L (elevated on current levothyroxine dose; dose increased from 75 mcg to 100 mcg); ALT 42 U/L (improved from prior); creatinine 1.2 mg/dL (stable for single kidney). Blood pressure: 136/84 mmHg (at goal). Cabozantinib continued at 40 mg/day. Next restaging in 3 months. Cancer present; disease stable on cabozantinib.

At age 64 years (approximately 6 months of cabozantinib therapy, second restaging on new regimen), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST. Cancer present. Response assessment per RECIST 1.1: STABLE DISEASE (SD) - maintained. Pulmonary metastases: right upper lobe 1.4 cm (stable); left lower lobe 1.1 cm (stable); left upper lobe 0.7 cm (stable). Right lower lobe previously irradiated lesion: 1.3 cm (stable). Hepatic segment VI lesion: 1.2 cm (stable). Right adrenal: 1.2 cm (stable). Sum of target lesion diameters: 6.1 cm (essentially unchanged from prior 6.2 cm). No new lesions. No bone metastases. Assessment: continued stable disease on cabozantinib at 6-month mark from initiation. Metastatic sites: PULMONARY (bilateral), HEPATIC (segment VI), ADRENAL (right).

At age 64 years (6 months on cabozantinib, 15+ months from initial diagnosis of metastatic disease), the patient had an oncologist visit for ongoing management. She is maintaining stable disease on cabozantinib 40 mg/day. Quality of life acceptable; she participates in light daily walks and assists with her grandchildren. Toxicities: persistent grade 1 hand-foot syndrome (right heel most affected, managed with silicone heel cups and urea cream); grade 1 diarrhea (1-2 stools above baseline, managed with dietary fiber modification; loperamide used occasionally); grade 1 fatigue. No new adverse events. No dose reductions currently needed. Labs: TSH 2.8 mIU/L (therapeutic on levothyroxine 100 mcg); CBC: Hgb 10.2 g/dL (mild anemia, stable); platelets 198,000/mcL; creatinine 1.2 mg/dL; LFTs normal. Blood pressure: 134/82 mmHg (at goal). Patient agreeable to continue current therapy. Goals of care discussion initiated: patient's priorities are quality of life, maintaining independence, and being present for family events. Palliative care integrated co-management arranged. Cancer present; stable disease on cabozantinib.

At age 64 years (approximately 9 months on cabozantinib), the patient had an oncologist office visit. She continues on cabozantinib 40 mg/day with ongoing stable disease. A new complaint of mild right hip discomfort was noted; bone scan ordered to exclude osseous metastasis (result: negative for bone metastasis; mild degenerative changes at right hip consistent with osteoarthritis). Ongoing toxicities: grade 1 HFS (stable, managed with supportive care); grade 1 diarrhea (managed); grade 1 fatigue. No new immune-related events (off immunotherapy for 9 months). Continued monitoring of blood pressure and thyroid function. TSH: 2.5 mIU/L (therapeutic). Levothyroxine continued at 100 mcg/day. Cabozantinib dose maintained. Restaging CT scheduled in 3 months. Cancer present (stable disease per last restaging). Overall disease trajectory: prolonged stable disease on cabozantinib with manageable toxicity profile, consistent with treatment goals discussed with patient and palliative care team.

documents.text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Cycle 2, Week 6 of Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (Yervoy/Opdivo)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Margaret Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) with sarcomatoid differentiation presenting today for her cycle 2 ipilimumab/nivolumab infusion and toxicity assessment. She is now approximately 6 weeks into first-line combination immune checkpoint therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo is well-known to our service. By way of brief background: she was diagnosed with IMDC intermediate-risk metastatic ccRCC involving bilateral pulmonary metastases and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, with a large right renal primary (11.4 cm) and infrarenal IVC thrombus. Pathology demonstrated WHO-ISUP grade 4 disease with approximately 22% sarcomatoid differentiation. Genomic profiling showed VHL and PBRM1 mutations, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and high PD-L1 expression (CPS ~55). She underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection and IVC thrombectomy approximately 10 weeks ago, which was technically successful with negative surgical margins, though final pathology confirmed 9 of 14 harvested lymph nodes positive for metastatic disease with extranodal extension. A pre-treatment baseline CT performed roughly 6 weeks post-operatively confirmed four bilateral pulmonary metastases (right lower lobe 2.1 cm, right upper lobe 1.2 cm, left lower lobe 0.9 cm, left upper lobe 0.7 cm) as the sole sites of residual measurable disease.

Approximately 8 weeks after surgery, she initiated first-line ipilimumab 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks per the CheckMate 214 schema, chosen given her intermediate IMDC risk category, sarcomatoid histology, and high PD-L1 expression. She completed cycle 1 without significant difficulty. Cycle 2 is being administered today.

Since cycle 1, she reports the following symptoms:

Fatigue: She describes a low-level fatigue that began within the first week of therapy and has been consistent since. She characterizes it as feeling "more tired than usual, but manageable." She is still able to perform light household tasks and short walks but has curtailed some of her usual activities. She grades this a 2-3 out of 10 in severity. This is consistent with Grade 1 immune-related or infusion-related fatigue.

Nausea: She reports intermittent mild nausea, predominantly in the first 4-5 days after her infusion. She has been using ondansetron 4 mg orally as needed with good effect. No vomiting. No difficulty maintaining oral intake. This is consistent with Grade 1 nausea, managed.

Bowel changes: She notes she has been having approximately 2 loose stools per day above her personal baseline (normally 1 well-formed stool per day; now 2-3 soft-to-loose stools, without urgency). She denies frank watery diarrhea. No blood or mucus in stool. No nocturnal awakening due to bowel urgency. No significant abdominal cramping. She does not describe this as debilitating. This is consistent with Grade 1 diarrhea. I have discussed with her the importance of monitoring carefully -- the threshold for concern is 4 or more stools above baseline per day, blood in stool, significant abdominal pain, or fever, at which point she should call our triage line immediately.

Skin: No rash. No pruritus. No hypopigmentation or vitiligo-like changes.

Musculoskeletal: No joint pain, stiffness, or swelling.

Respiratory: She denies new or worsening shortness of breath, cough, or chest discomfort. She had baseline mild exertional dyspnea attributed to her pulmonary metastases; this is not worsening.

Endocrine: She denies new symptoms of palpitations, tremor, heat intolerance, increased sweating, or new-onset constipation beyond the above-noted bowel changes. No headache, visual changes, or other symptoms to suggest hypophysitis.

Neurological: No new headache, diplopia, facial droop, focal weakness or numbness, or cognitive changes.

Cardiovascular: No chest pain, new palpitations, or peripheral edema.

Hypertension: Her blood pressure at home has been running around 130/80 mmHg per her home log. She is maintained on amlodipine 10 mg/day for pre-existing hypertension.

Pain: She denies significant pain. The post-surgical right flank discomfort has largely resolved. She occasionally uses acetaminophen for minor musculoskeletal aches. No back pain or bone pain.

Appetite: Mildly reduced appetite, particularly around the time of infusion, but maintaining weight and oral intake.

Psychosocial: She reports feeling cautiously optimistic about treatment. Her adult daughter accompanies her today. She has been in good communication with our nursing team via the portal.

She has NOT developed any symptoms consistent with immune-related adverse events requiring systemic corticosteroids at this time.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: Fatigue (Grade 1), nausea (Grade 1, controlled with ondansetron), loose stools 2 above baseline (Grade 1 diarrhea). Mildly reduced appetite.
Negative: No fever, no chills, no significant weight loss. No rash, pruritis, or skin blistering. No arthralgias or myalgias. No dyspnea at rest or new-onset dyspnea. No cough. No pleuritic chest pain. No palpitations. No diplopia, ptosis, or visual changes. No headache. No dysuria. No hematuria. No edema. No new lymphadenopathy perceived by patient. No jaundice. No right upper quadrant pain.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma, right kidney, Stage IV (cT3bN2M1) -- see above
2. Post right radical nephrectomy, retroperitoneal lymph node dissection, IVC thrombectomy
3. Hypertension -- on amlodipine 10 mg
4. Hyperlipidemia -- on rosuvastatin
5. Remote tobacco use history: 20 pack-years, quit at age 48

SOCIAL HISTORY
Retired secondary school administrator. Lives with adult daughter who assists with transportation to appointments. No current tobacco or alcohol use. No illicit drug use. Sedentary-to-light activity level currently given treatment-related fatigue. Of West African descent.

FAMILY HISTORY
Not previously documented in detail in our notes. Patient reports a maternal uncle with kidney disease (uncertain if malignant); no known family history of hereditary cancer syndromes. Germline testing from tumor NGS panel showed no pathogenic variants in VHL, SDHA/B/C/D, FH, TSC1/2, MET, or FLCN, making a hereditary RCC syndrome very unlikely.

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS
1. Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
2. Rosuvastatin 20 mg orally once daily (hyperlipidemia)
3. Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg orally as needed (pain)
4. Ondansetron 4 mg orally every 8 hours as needed (nausea) -- prescribed at cycle 1 start
5. Ipilimumab 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (today is cycle 2)
6. Nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (today is cycle 2)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 132/80 mmHg, heart rate 76 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 36.8 C, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 74.2 kg (stable from baseline).
ECOG Performance Status: 1.

General: Ms. Okonkwo is a well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Conversational and engaged.

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light. Extraocular movements intact. Oropharynx: moist mucous membranes, no oral mucositis, no thrush. No thyroid enlargement or tenderness on palpation.

Neck: Supple. No cervical, supraclavicular, or infraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mrs. Okonkwo was originally diagnosed with Stage IV (cT3bN2M1) clear cell RCC of the right kidney approximately five months ago. Her presentation included an 11.4 cm right renal mass with IVC thrombus extension (infrarenal, non-occlusive), bulky retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy with multiple involved nodes in the aortocaval and para-aortic regions, and bilateral pulmonary metastases (four lesions, largest 1.9 cm in the right lower lobe). Sarcomatoid differentiation was identified in approximately 20-22% of the sampled tumor. PD-L1 by 22C3 assay was markedly elevated with a CPS of approximately 55. Comprehensive genomic profiling demonstrated VHL and PBRM1 as co-drivers, with additional SETD2 frameshift and CDKN2A deletion. TMB was intermediate-low at 6.2 mut/Mb; MSS.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board review, she underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with RPLND and IVC thrombectomy. Surgical pathology confirmed pT3bN2 ccRCC with 9 of 14 lymph nodes involved, extranodal extension in the largest node (3.1 cm), and IVC thrombus measuring 4.2 cm with clear IVC wall margins. The sarcomatoid component was present in the lymph node metastases as well.

She initiated first-line combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab (ipi) 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks plus nivolumab (nivo) 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks approximately 8 weeks post-operatively, following a baseline restaging CT that showed four bilateral pulmonary nodules as the sole sites of evaluable disease (sum of diameters 5.0 cm, per baseline established on that post-op scan).

Treatment course summary:
- Cycle 1 (ipi/nivo): Tolerated well. Grade 1 fatigue, grade 1 nausea managed with PRN ondansetron, grade 1 loose stools.
- Cycle 2 (ipi/nivo): Administered without dose modification. Ongoing grade 1 GI symptoms and fatigue. TSH normal at cycle 2 check.
- Cycle 3 (ipi/nivo): Complicated by immune-related colitis, CTCAE Grade 2 — she developed 6-8 watery stools per day with lower abdominal cramping. Colonoscopy confirmed immune-mediated colitis with increased intraepithelial lymphocytes and crypt distortion on biopsy. Ipilimumab was held for one cycle; nivolumab was continued with caution. She was started on oral budesonide 9 mg daily. Symptoms improved to grade 1 within approximately 10 days. She was negative for infectious causes on stool cultures.
- Cycle 4 (ipi/nivo, administered with a 1-cycle delay): Ipilimumab was reintroduced after colitis resolved to grade 1 or less. She completed the fourth and final ipilimumab dose.

First restaging CT (performed approximately 12 weeks after starting ipi/nivo, i.e., approximately 3 months into therapy) showed a very encouraging partial response: all four pulmonary nodules shrank substantially. The right lower lobe lesion went from 2.1 cm to 1.3 cm, the right upper lobe from 1.2 cm to 0.7 cm, the left lower lobe from 0.9 cm to 0.5 cm, and the left upper lobe from 0.7 cm to 0.4 cm. The combined sum of the four target lesions dropped from 5.0 cm at baseline to approximately 2.9 cm — a reduction of roughly 42%. Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy was not identified on restaging. No new sites of disease.

She presents today to review the scan results and formally transition to nivolumab 480 mg flat-dose IV every 4 weeks (maintenance, per CheckMate 214 protocol).

**REASON FOR MULTIDISCIPLINARY REVIEW**
Margaret Okonkwo is a 64-year-old woman (63 at time of initial diagnosis) with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), pT3bN2M1, with sarcomatoid differentiation (~22%), presenting today for formal MDT reassessment following an interval restaging CT that demonstrates a mixed response pattern on first-line ipilimumab/nivolumab. She has been on therapy for approximately 6.5 months total (4 cycles of ipilimumab 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg induction, followed by nivolumab 480 mg every 4 weeks maintenance for the last ~2 months). The case was reviewed in the context of new imaging showing enlargement of the right lower lobe pulmonary lesion and the appearance of a new right adrenal nodule, while the remaining pulmonary lesions remain essentially stable.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**

Ms. Okonkwo was initially diagnosed with Stage IV (cT3bN2M1) clear cell RCC approximately 6.5 months ago following evaluation of a right flank mass identified incidentally on imaging obtained for an unrelated complaint. Her staging workup revealed an 11.4 cm right renal primary with extension into the right renal vein and approximately 3 cm of infrarenal IVC thrombus (non-occlusive), bulky retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (right para-aortic nodes to 2.8 cm, aortocaval 2.2 cm, right common iliac chain 1.5 cm), and bilateral pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe dominant lesion 1.9 cm, right upper lobe 1.1 cm, left lower lobe 0.8 cm, left upper lobe 0.6 cm). No hepatic, osseous, or brain metastases were identified on initial staging. MRI brain was negative at diagnosis.

Core needle biopsy confirmed ccRCC, Fuhrman/WHO-ISUP grade 3-4, with approximately 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, high PD-L1 expression (CPS ~55 by 22C3 assay), and loss of PBRM1 expression by IHC. Comprehensive genomic profiling revealed pathogenic VHL, PBRM1, and SETD2 mutations, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and intermediate-low TMB (6.2 mut/Mb); microsatellite stable/pMMR. IMDC intermediate-risk classification (2 factors: hemoglobin below LLN and Karnofsky PS <80 at nadir during initial workup).

The GU MDT recommended cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection and IVC thrombectomy, followed by ipilimumab/nivolumab, given the combination of IMDC intermediate risk, sarcomatoid features, and high PD-L1 — circumstances that strongly favor dual checkpoint blockade based on the CheckMate 214 sarcomatoid subgroup and pooled analyses demonstrating meaningful OS benefit in this population.

She underwent open right radical nephrectomy approximately 8 weeks prior to starting systemic therapy. The final pathology confirmed pT3bN2, 11.8 cm primary, 22% sarcomatoid differentiation, 9/14 nodes positive (largest 3.1 cm with extranodal extension), negative margins. IVC thrombus was Level I-II, 4.2 cm; IVC wall margins negative. Post-operative course was uncomplicated aside from a brief ileus on POD2.

Pre-treatment baseline CT (6 weeks post-nephrectomy) showed bilateral pulmonary nodules minimally increased from staging: RLL 2.1 cm, RUL 1.2 cm, LLL 0.9 cm, LUL 0.7 cm. No new sites of disease at that time.

Systemic therapy with ipilimumab (1 mg/kg q3w x4) plus nivolumab (3 mg/kg q3w x4 induction, then 480 mg q4w maintenance) was initiated approximately 8 weeks post-surgery.

Course of therapy:
- Cycle 1: tolerated well. Mild fatigue, nausea (G1), grade 1 diarrhea.
- Cycle 3 (approximately week 10): developed grade 2 immune-related colitis — 6-8 loose/watery stools per day, confirmed on colonoscopy (diffuse mucosal edema, intraepithelial lymphocytes, crypt distortion). Ipilimumab held; oral budesonide initiated at 9 mg/day. Nivolumab continued. Resolved to grade 1 within ~10 days. Ipilimumab reinitiated at the subsequent cycle.
- First restaging CT (~12 weeks): partial response — all four pulmonary lesions decreased. RLL from 2.1 to 1.3 cm, RUL from 1.2 to 0.7 cm, LLL from 0.9 to 0.5 cm, LUL from 0.7 to 0.4 cm. Sum of diameters 2.9 cm from 5.0 cm pre-treatment (-42%).
- Completed 4 ipilimumab doses (4th dose with approximately 1-cycle delay due to the colitis hold) and transitioned to nivolumab 480 mg q4w maintenance.
- Approximately 5 months into therapy: developed grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism (TSH 18.4 mIU/L, free T4 0.6 ng/dL, TPO antibodies positive). Levothyroxine initiated, titrated to 75 mcg/day with normalization of TSH. Nivolumab continued without interruption.

Most recent restaging CT (~6 months, second restaging on this regimen): demonstrates a MIXED RESPONSE pattern:
- RLL pulmonary lesion: increased from 1.3 cm to 2.4 cm (nearly doubling from nadir, +85% from nadir measurement)
- RUL: 0.6 cm (stable/minimal decrease from 0.7 cm)
- LLL: 0.5 cm (stable)
- LUL: 0.4 cm (stable)
- NEW right adrenal nodule: 1.3 cm, not present on baseline CT, suspicious for adrenal metastasis
- No hepatic, osseous, or new pulmonary nodules beyond the four previously identified
- No intracranial disease (brain imaging not repeated at this scan given stability; to be discussed)

Sum of diameters of target lesions has increased from nadir 2.9 cm to 3.9 cm (+34% from nadir), driven largely by the RLL lesion.

The patient reports she is functionally feeling reasonably well. She notices that she is more fatigued than she was during the first three months of therapy. She has had intermittent mild right flank/lower back discomfort but no localizing symptoms suggesting osseous or adrenal involvement (no flank pain, no hypertensive crisis, no symptoms of Cushing's). Bowel habits are near baseline (1-2 loose stools/day, grade 1). Thyroid symptoms resolved. No dyspnea at rest, mild exertional dyspnea with brisk walking, which she attributes to deconditioning and mild anemia.

---

**IMAGING SUMMARY (FOR MDT DISCUSSION)**

Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (~6-month restaging, second on-therapy restaging):
- Pulmonary: The dominant right lower lobe lesion that initially responded to ipi/nivo has now rebounded significantly — from its nadir of 1.3 cm it has grown back to 2.4 cm, which is nearly back to its pretreatment size of 2.1 cm and arguably represents true disease progression at that site. The remaining three pulmonary lesions (RUL, LLL, LUL) remain small and are essentially unchanged from the first restaging scan.
- Adrenal: A new, rounded 1.3 cm right adrenal nodule is present. Given the oncologic history and morphology, adrenal metastasis must be considered. Adrenal cortical adenoma remains in the differential, but the new appearance in the setting of progressive ccRCC warrants tissue confirmation prior to local therapy.
- Retroperitoneum: No clearly enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes post-RPLND. Post-surgical fibrotic changes in the right retroperitoneum without discrete nodal recurrence.
- Liver, bones, soft tissue: No hepatic metastases at this time. No osseous lesions.
- Previous nephrectomy bed: No evidence of local recurrence.

Overall radiographic pattern: Oligoprogression at two discrete sites (dominant RLL pulmonary lesion, new right adrenal nodule) with concurrent stability at the remaining three pulmonary metastatic sites. This is a classic oligoprogression pattern in the checkpoint inhibitor setting.

---

**MDT ATTENDEES AND DISCUSSION**

In attendance: Medical Oncology (GU), Thoracic Radiation Oncology, Interventional Radiology, Urology/Surgical Oncology, Diagnostic Radiology, and Palliative Care Integration.

The imaging was reviewed in detail by the multidisciplinary team. The following points were deliberated:

1. **Nature of the mixed response**: Three of the four pulmonary lesions remain small and stable or minimally improved, suggesting that nivolumab maintenance may be providing ongoing disease control at those sites. The RLL lesion, however, has grown back substantially from nadir, and the right adrenal nodule is new. This pattern is consistent with oligoprogression — progression at a limited number of sites while most disease remains controlled.

2. **Significance of the adrenal nodule**: The adrenal nodule needs to be confirmed as metastatic before any local therapy (SBRT or adrenalectomy) is directed at it. Adrenal cortical adenomas are common in this age group and can have a similar CT appearance. Additionally, functional adrenal assessment (plasma metanephrines) is warranted given the proximity to prior retroperitoneal surgery and the theoretical possibility of a functioning lesion, though the morphology favors a non-functional process.

3. **Management options reviewed**:
   Option A: Continue nivolumab maintenance, deliver SBRT to the RLL pulmonary lesion (ablative dose), obtain CT-guided biopsy of the right adrenal nodule — if confirmed metastatic, consider adrenal SBRT as well. This approach allows preservation of the systemic immunotherapy benefit for the sites that are responding, while controlling the resistant clone locally.
   Option B: Discontinue nivolumab entirely and switch to second-line VEGFR TKI therapy (most likely cabozantinib given its activity post-IO and in patients with sarcomatoid features and bone/soft tissue disease). Risk: lose systemic immunotherapy control of stable lesions.
   Option C: Continue nivolumab alone, repeat imaging in 6-8 weeks to assess trajectory. Risk: further growth of progressing lesions while awaiting repeat imaging.
   Option D: Add a TKI to nivolumab (e.g., lenvatinib + pembrolizumab switch, or nivolumab + cabozantinib off-label). This is less favored given it represents a deviation from the current treatment framework and has not been formally studied as a "switch" strategy post-partial-mixed-response to ipi/nivo.

4. **Decision reached**: Given that only 2 sites appear to be driving progression while 3 lesions are stable or responding, the MDT consensus is to pursue Option A — **oligoprogression management strategy**:
   - Proceed with **CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right adrenal nodule** (Interventional Radiology to schedule) to confirm metastatic ccRCC.
   - Assuming biopsy confirms metastatic disease, plan **SBRT to the right lower lobe pulmonary metastasis** (2.4 cm). Thoracic radiation oncology agrees the lesion is technically accessible and well-suited to SBRT (54 Gy in 3 fractions is the anticipated prescription). 4D-CT simulation with respiratory gating will be used given the pulmonary location.
   - **Nivolumab to be continued** during the adrenal biopsy workup, and the SBRT week will have nivolumab held (to resume the week after completion).
   - If adrenal biopsy confirms ccRCC metastasis, SBRT to the right adrenal gland will be offered, or laparoscopic adrenalectomy can be considered depending on patient preference and surgical assessment; this decision deferred pending biopsy result.
   - Close interval restaging CT in approximately 8-10 weeks following completion of local therapy to assess the overall systemic response trajectory. If the remaining lesions begin to grow after local consolidation, second-line systemic therapy with cabozantinib will be initiated at that time.
   - No changes to levothyroxine or other supportive medications at this time.

5. **Brain MRI**: Given 9 months of systemic therapy and mixed response picture, an updated MRI brain with contrast should be obtained prior to or concurrent with the adrenal biopsy. Although the patient has no neurologic symptoms, clear cell RCC with sarcomatoid features and progressive systemic disease warrants updated intracranial staging.

---

**ASSESSMENT**

1. Metastatic clear cell RCC, pT3bN2M1, with sarcomatoid differentiation (~22%), IMDC intermediate risk — approximately 6.5 months into first-line ipilimumab/nivolumab therapy.
2. Mixed radiographic response: initial partial response confirmed at 3 months, now with oligoprogression — dominant right lower lobe pulmonary metastasis has grown back to 2.4 cm and a new right adrenal nodule (1.3 cm) has appeared, while the remaining pulmonary lesions remain small and stable.
3. Immune-related adverse events (history): grade 2 colitis (resolved, off budesonide now), grade 2 hypothyroidism (managed with levothyroxine 75 mcg/day, TSH therapeutic).
4. Performance status ECOG 1, functionally well, tolerating therapy.
5. Mild anemia (Hgb 10.1 g/dL), stable; attributed to chronic disease and prior surgical blood loss. LFTs normal; LDH mildly elevated, trending up.

---

**PLAN**

1. **CT-guided core needle biopsy of right adrenal nodule**: Scheduled with Interventional Radiology. Pre-procedure plasma metanephrines to exclude functional lesion. Pathology to confirm metastatic ccRCC; given intact sarcomatoid history, will also request repeat IHC panel (PAX8, CA9, CD10) on adrenal specimen.

2. **MRI brain with and without gadolinium**: Order prior to SBRT planning to update intracranial staging.

3. **SBRT planning for right lower lobe pulmonary metastasis (2.4 cm)**: Thoracic radiation oncology team will initiate planning. Target dose 54 Gy in 3 fractions; 4D-CT simulation with respiratory gating. Nivolumab to be held for the treatment week only, then resumed.

4. **Continue nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks** while local therapy arrangements are underway. No dose modification warranted at this time.

5. **Adrenal management**: Following biopsy result, will reconvene to discuss SBRT versus surgical adrenalectomy for right adrenal metastasis. Surgical Oncology/Urology to provide assessment of laparoscopic adrenalectomy feasibility given prior right-sided retroperitoneal surgery.

6. **Labs at next visit**: CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH. Monitor LDH as a surrogate marker of systemic tumor burden.

7. **Restaging imaging**: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 8-10 weeks (approximately 2 months after local therapy completion) to assess systemic disease status. If systemic progression is confirmed at that point, will transition to cabozantinib.

8. **Palliative care**: Ongoing co-management with palliative care team. Goals of care previously discussed; patient's priority remains quality of life and family time. She understands the current plan and agrees to the biopsy and radiation therapy approach.

9. **Patient counseling**: Lengthy discussion with the patient and her adult daughter following MDT deliberations. The concept of oligoprogression was explained in lay terms — that most of the cancer lesions are still being controlled by her current immunotherapy, but one lung spot and a new spot on the adrenal gland appear to be growing or newly present, so we want to treat those specific spots with targeted radiation while keeping the immunotherapy working for the rest. The patient expressed understanding and agreement with the plan. She has no objections to the adrenal biopsy and is motivated to proceed.

**Return to clinic**: 2-3 weeks (post-adrenal biopsy) for biopsy result review, SBRT coordination, and overall treatment status assessment.

Mrs. Margaret Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman with known stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) with sarcomatoid differentiation, who presents today for a scheduled restaging visit following CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and MRI brain performed earlier this week. HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mrs. Okonkwo has been followed in our clinic since she was diagnosed with metastatic ccRCC presenting with an 11.4 cm right renal mass, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (aortocaval nodes up to 2.8 cm), and bilateral pulmonary metastases. At the time of diagnosis, sarcomatoid differentiation comprised approximately 20% of the biopsy specimen, and PD-L1 expression was high (CPS 55 by 22C3). NGS from the biopsy demonstrated a VHL p.R161* nonsense mutation, PBRM1 splice-site loss-of-function mutation, SETD2 frameshift mutation, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion — a genomic profile consistent with biologically aggressive ccRCC. IMDC risk was classified as intermediate (two factors: Karnofsky <80 at nadir during workup, hemoglobin below LLN at presentation).

She underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection and IVC thrombectomy approximately 8 weeks prior to starting systemic therapy. Surgical pathology confirmed pT3bN2 disease with 9 of 14 lymph nodes positive, including extranodal extension, and an IVC thrombus level I-II. Post-operatively, she recovered well, with only a brief post-operative ileus.

She initiated first-line immunotherapy with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg IV q3 weeks and nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV q3 weeks (CheckMate 214 schedule) approximately 9 months ago. Treatment was notable for:
- Grade 2 immune-related colitis at cycle 3, requiring ipilimumab hold and oral budesonide; this resolved with treatment and ipilimumab was eventually completed for all 4 induction doses with a 1-cycle delay.
- Transition to nivolumab (Opdivo) 480 mg IV q4 weeks maintenance after completing 4 induction cycles. First restaging at 3 months showed a meaningful partial response — all four pulmonary lesions decreased by approximately 40-44%.

Her second restaging at ~6 months revealed a mixed response: most pulmonary lesions remained stable, but the right lower lobe lesion grew significantly (1.3 cm to 2.4 cm) and a new 1.3 cm right adrenal nodule appeared. She underwent SBRT to the right lower lobe pulmonary lesion (54 Gy in 3 fractions) approximately 1.5 months ago, with nivolumab briefly held during that week and then resumed.

She now presents for the third restaging scan, performed approximately 9 months after starting first-line immunotherapy. The results are reviewed in detail below.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mrs. Margaret Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman with IMDC intermediate-risk metastatic clear cell RCC with sarcomatoid features (22%), presenting today with confirmation of systemic disease progression on nivolumab (Opdivo) maintenance therapy.

1. DISEASE STATUS — SYSTEMIC PROGRESSION ON FIRST-LINE IMMUNOTHERAPY
Today's imaging confirms that despite the SBRT achieving local control of the treated right lower lobe pulmonary lesion, the remaining pulmonary lesions have grown substantially (right upper lobe: 3x increase; left lower lobe: nearly 3x increase; left upper lobe: more than 2x increase). More significantly, a new hepatic metastasis has appeared in segment VI. The right adrenal lesion (previously biopsy-confirmed ccRCC) has also grown slightly. This is clearly systemic progression and not a pattern amenable to further local consolidation — there are simply too many active sites progressing simultaneously for local therapy alone to be a viable strategy.

The overall trajectory is: partial response at 3 months on ipi/nivo, then mixed response at 6 months, local SBRT at 7.5 months, and now unambiguous systemic progression at 9 months. She received approximately 9 months of total immunotherapy benefit before progression. This is meaningful but insufficient for long-term disease control, and her high-risk genomic features (CDKN2A deletion, SETD2 mutation, 22% sarcomatoid component) are consistent with the biological aggressiveness of her disease.

Plan: DISCONTINUE nivolumab effective today. No further immunotherapy at this time.

2. SECOND-LINE SYSTEMIC THERAPY — CABOZANTINIB
After discussing the case in the context of available evidence and current NCCN guidelines, the next line of therapy will be cabozantinib (Cabometyx), a multi-targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitor with activity against VEGFR1/2/3, MET, AXL, and RET. The METEOR trial demonstrated a significant overall survival benefit for cabozantinib versus everolimus in the second-line setting after VEGF-directed therapy, and several datasets now show robust activity after prior immunotherapy — including the CONTACT-03 data and real-world experience. Cabozantinib inhibits MET and AXL, which are pathways implicated in sarcomatoid dedifferentiation, providing additional biological rationale in this patient's setting.

Starting dose: Cabozantinib 60 mg orally once daily (label dose). Given that she does not currently have significant hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea beyond grade 1, or hepatic dysfunction requiring dose modification, starting at the full 60 mg dose is appropriate, with close monitoring.

Counseling provided for expected toxicities:
- Hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia): Most common at weeks 2-6. Counseled to use urea-based cream daily on palms and soles prophylactically, wear well-cushioned footwear, avoid heat and friction. She has no current HFS given she is not on a TKI.
- Diarrhea: Likely to worsen from her current mild baseline. Instructed to use loperamide 4 mg first dose then 2 mg after each loose stool, and to call clinic if more than 4-5 stools above baseline per day.
- Hypertension: She is already on amlodipine 10 mg for pre-existing hypertension. BP is currently 136/86 mmHg — within acceptable range. She will need close BP monitoring q1-2 weeks initially, with the expectation that cabozantinib will likely worsen her BP. Standing orders placed for BP checks and lisinopril initiation if BP exceeds 140/90 on repeat measurements (lisinopril also has nephroprotective benefit in her single-kidney setting).
- Fatigue: Expected; encourage adequate rest and gentle activity.
- Hepatotoxicity: Baseline LFTs are mildly elevated (AST 52, ALT 48), likely reflecting the new hepatic metastasis rather than drug effect at this point. LFTs will be rechecked in 3-4 weeks.
- Hypothyroidism/thyroid effects: She is already on levothyroxine 100 mcg for checkpoint inhibitor-associated hypothyroidism. TSH currently well-controlled. Will continue to monitor — cabozantinib can also affect thyroid function.
- Nausea: PRN ondansetron provided.

Prescription for cabozantinib 60 mg daily sent to specialty pharmacy. Oral chemotherapy teaching provided by the clinic nurse and pharmacist today. Patient verbalized understanding.

3. HEPATIC METASTASIS
The new hepatic segment VI lesion (1.6 cm) is a new site of disease, first appearing on today's scan. The hypervascular appearance on arterial phase is consistent with ccRCC metastasis. In the context of systemic progression with multiple other active sites, pursuing hepatic SBRT or hepatic directed therapy to this single lesion would not be the appropriate strategy — she has concomitant pulmonary and adrenal progression. This lesion will be followed with systemic therapy; we expect cabozantinib will address the hepatic disease as part of systemic treatment.

4. RIGHT ADRENAL METASTASIS
The confirmed right adrenal metastasis (biopsied last month, confirmed ccRCC) measures 1.5 cm — slight increase from 1.3 cm. Although SBRT to the adrenal was discussed at the MDT earlier this month, the emergence of new hepatic disease and the systemic progression across multiple pulmonary sites argues against local adrenal therapy in isolation. This will be managed as part of systemic therapy. We will re-evaluate in 3 months whether any local strategy for the adrenal metastasis is warranted depending on cabozantinib response.

5. ANEMIA
Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, normocytic — mild anemia of chronic disease/cancer. No evidence of active bleeding. Iron studies and reticulocyte count to be checked at the next lab draw. No indication for transfusion at this time. Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents not appropriate in this setting; will monitor. Fatigue is multifactorial (disease burden, anemia, prior treatment).

6. MILDLY ELEVATED LIVER FUNCTION TESTS
AST 52, ALT 48, alkaline phosphatase 124 — all mildly elevated above normal. In the context of a new hepatic metastasis, this is most likely tumor-related. There is no clinical picture of hepatocellular injury pattern (bilirubin normal, no jaundice). Checkpoint inhibitor hepatitis is a consideration, though she received her last nivolumab dose over 3 weeks ago and the trend from prior labs (which were normal) to now coincides with appearance of the new hepatic lesion, making metastatic disease the more likely etiology. We will recheck LFTs in 3-4 weeks after starting cabozantinib; if LFTs worsen beyond grade 2 on the new agent, further workup (imaging review, possible liver biopsy) will be considered.

7. HYPOTHYROIDISM — STABLE
TSH 2.4 mIU/L on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily — well-controlled. No changes to thyroid management today. Levothyroxine to be continued indefinitely given checkpoint inhibitor-associated thyroid destruction.

8. HYPERTENSION
BP 136/86 mmHg on amlodipine 10 mg — at the upper acceptable range at baseline before starting cabozantinib. As noted above, will monitor closely and anticipate need for additional antihypertensive therapy. Standing orders for lisinopril 10 mg if BP rises above 140/90 mmHg on repeat measurement.

9. ECOG PERFORMANCE STATUS
ECOG PS 1. Patient is ambulatory, capable of self-care, and active for >50% of waking hours. She remains a candidate for systemic therapy and is motivated to continue treatment.

10. GOALS OF CARE AND PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT
We had a brief but important conversation today about the trajectory of her illness. Mrs. Okonkwo understands that her cancer has progressed on immunotherapy and that we are moving to a different class of therapy. She is aware that this is metastatic disease and that the goal of treatment is to control the cancer and maintain quality of life, not to cure. She is emotionally resilient but noted she was disappointed to hear the disease had grown. She continues to live with her adult daughter and maintains close family ties. Her primary stated priorities remain: quality of life, maintaining independence, and being present for her family and grandchildren. She is open to palliative care co-management, which remains in place. Social work referral offered — patient declined today but is aware the offer stands.

Return to clinic in 3-4 weeks (or sooner PRN) for toxicity assessment and lab check on cabozantinib. Repeat restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 3 months to assess response. MRI brain will be repeated if neurological symptoms develop.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Okonkwo is a 64-year-old female with a known diagnosis of Stage IV (cT3bN2M1) clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation (22% of tumor volume), initially presenting with an 11.4 cm right renal mass with renal vein and infrarenal IVC involvement (Level I-II thrombus), retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (9/14 nodes positive at resection), and bilateral pulmonary metastases. Germline testing was negative for hereditary RCC syndromes. Molecular profiling demonstrated VHL loss-of-function mutation, PBRM1 splice-site mutation, SETD2 frameshift mutation, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion; PD-L1 CPS was 55 (high) by 22C3 immunohistochemistry. IMDC intermediate-risk group.

She underwent open cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection and IVC thrombectomy without major complication. Final surgical pathology confirmed pT3b N2 (9/14 positive) ccRCC, WHO/ISUP grade 4, sarcomatoid features 22%, with extranodal extension in the largest involved node.

Approximately 8 weeks post-operatively, she began first-line systemic therapy with Yervoy (1 mg/kg IV q3w x 4 doses) plus Opdivo (3 mg/kg IV q3w for induction, then Opdivo 480 mg IV q4w for maintenance), per the CheckMate 214 regimen. Her course was complicated by immune-related grade 2 colitis (managed with held ipi and oral budesonide, which ultimately resolved) and immune-related grade 2 hypothyroidism (managed with levothyroxine, now continued indefinitely at 100 mcg/day). She achieved a partial response at first restaging (approximately -42% sum of target lesion diameters).

At approximately 6 months of therapy, she developed mixed response with enlargement of the right lower lobe pulmonary lesion and appearance of a new right adrenal nodule. CT-guided biopsy of the adrenal confirmed metastatic ccRCC. She underwent SBRT to the right lower lobe lesion (54 Gy in 3 fractions) with local control achieved on subsequent imaging. However, at restaging approximately 9 months into first-line therapy, systemic progressive disease was documented: multiple pulmonary lesions had grown, a new hepatic segment VI lesion appeared (1.6 cm), and the adrenal lesion had slightly increased. She was transitioned to second-line therapy with cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 60 mg/day.

Cabozantinib was dose-reduced to 40 mg/day after approximately 3 weeks due to concurrent Grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (HFS) and Grade 2 diarrhea. Antihypertensive regimen was augmented (lisinopril 10 mg/day added to amlodipine 10 mg/day) for Grade 2 hypertension developing at approximately 6 weeks on cabozantinib. At her 3-month restaging on cabozantinib, imaging demonstrated stable disease with modest reductions across all measurable sites (approximately -16% from cabozantinib initiation). At 6-month restaging, stable disease was maintained with all lesions unchanged. She has now been on cabozantinib for approximately 9 months.

Today's visit is prompted by a NEW complaint of mild right hip discomfort beginning approximately 3-4 weeks ago. She describes a dull, achy pain localized to the right groin and lateral hip region, rated approximately 2-3/10 at baseline, worsening to 4-5/10 with prolonged ambulation. There is no radiation down the leg, no associated weakness or numbness, no mechanical instability, and no history of trauma. She has not required opioid analgesics. She has been taking acetaminophen 500 mg BID with moderate benefit. Given her known metastatic disease to multiple sites, osseous metastasis was considered in the differential and a bone scan was ordered at this visit. She denies any other new musculoskeletal complaints.

With respect to her known metastatic sites and cabozantinib tolerability, she continues on cabozantinib 40 mg/day without further dose reductions since the initial adjustment. 1. Metastatic Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma (cT3bN2M1, Stage IV) — Currently on Second-Line Cabozantinib 40 mg Daily, Ongoing Stable Disease

Mrs. Okonkwo has now been on cabozantinib for approximately 9 months since the documented systemic progression on first-line ipilimumab/nivolumab. Her most recent restaging imaging at 6 months confirmed continued stable disease, with minor tumor decrements across all measured sites: bilateral pulmonary nodules, the previously SBRT-treated right lower lobe lesion, the segment VI hepatic metastasis, and the right adrenal metastasis. The overall trajectory is gratifying. She has tolerated a prolonged course of TKI therapy with manageable toxicities, and her performance status has remained ECOG 1 throughout. The patient continues to derive clinical benefit and maintains functional independence.

Plan: Continue cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily. No dose escalation indicated given prior grade 2 toxicity at 60 mg. The pattern of uptake is consistent with mild osteoarthritis at the right hip, correlating well with the lateral hip/greater trochanteric distribution of her pain and the lack of true hip joint restriction on examination. This is consistent with degenerative disease expected in a 64-year-old woman.

Plan: No further oncologic workup needed for this complaint at this time. Reassure patient that bone scan is negative for cancer involvement. Recommend NSAIDs with caution given single kidney and ongoing antihypertensive therapy (prefer acetaminophen 650 mg TID for analgesic effect in the short term; avoid NSAIDs if possible). Orthopedic or physiatry referral discussed; patient will trial conservative management first with supportive measures. Silicone trochanteric pad/hip support for lateral hip pain. Plan: Continue current approach. No dose modification for grade 1 diarrhea. Educate patient to contact clinic if diarrhea escalates to ≥4-6 stools/day above baseline (which would trigger dose hold per protocol).

Symptomatically, she has no recurrence of fatigue attributable to hypothyroidism as distinct from disease/treatment fatigue. Cabozantinib can also contribute to hypothyroidism or worsen existing disease, and monitoring has appropriately been maintained.

Plan: Continue levothyroxine 100 mcg/day. Repeat TSH in 3 months at next visit. No change at this time.

Blood pressure 134/82 mmHg today, meeting our target of <140/90 mmHg (and ideally <130/80 given single kidney with trace proteinuria and ongoing lisinopril use). On amlodipine 10 mg daily and lisinopril 10 mg daily. Potassium 4.1 mEq/L (normal; important to monitor on RAAS inhibitor).

8. Mild Anemia (Anemia of Chronic Disease / Cancer)

Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, stable and above transfusion threshold. MCV normocytic. No evidence of iron deficiency or vitamin deficiency at this time. This is a chronic, expected finding in the setting of metastatic malignancy and prior surgery.

Plan: Monitor closely. If symptomatic anemia develops (dyspnea at rest, significant fatigue, lightheadedness), consider further workup (iron studies, B12/folate, reticulocyte count) and potential erythropoiesis-stimulating agent discussion in accordance with ASCO guidelines. No intervention today.

9. Goals of Care and Psychosocial Support

At our prior visit, we had an initial goals-of-care conversation and arranged integration with palliative care. The patient has articulated clearly that her priorities are quality of life, maintaining independence, and being present for meaningful family events (grandchildren, family gatherings). If disease progresses on cabozantinib in the future, we will discuss available options including enrollment in clinical trials, belzutifan (HIF-2α inhibitor, approved third-line after IO and TKI), everolimus-based combinations, and other investigational strategies—but that conversation is premature given current stability.

MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old female with newly diagnosed right renal cell carcinoma, clear cell type, presenting as stage IV disease with bilateral pulmonary metastases and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. MRI brain obtained as part of complete staging workup to evaluate for intracranial metastatic involvement prior to systemic and/or surgical treatment planning.

COMPARISON: No prior brain MRI available for comparison.

TECHNIQUE: Multisequence MRI of the brain performed on a 3.0 Tesla magnet. Sequences acquired include sagittal T1, axial T2, axial FLAIR, axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping, axial gradient echo (GRE/susceptibility-weighted imaging), axial T1-weighted pre-contrast, and axial and coronal T1-weighted post-contrast sequences following intravenous administration of gadobutrol 0.1 mmol/kg. No acute complications from contrast administration.

---

FINDINGS:

BRAIN PARENCHYMA:
There are no intra-axial or extra-axial enhancing lesions to suggest intracranial metastatic disease. No masses identified in the supratentorial or infratentorial compartments. No parenchymal lesions demonstrating restricted diffusion on DWI/ADC to suggest acute ischemic infarction. The cortical gray matter and deep white matter appear symmetric in signal without focal signal abnormality in the cerebellum, brainstem, or deep gray structures.

WHITE MATTER:
Scattered foci of T2/FLAIR hyperintensity are present in the periventricular and subcortical white matter bilaterally, most prominent in the frontal and parietal regions. These changes are non-enhancing, without associated mass effect or restricted diffusion, and are overall mild in degree. Given the patient's age of 63 years, these findings are interpreted as age-appropriate chronic microvascular white matter changes (Fazekas grade 1-2). No evidence of demyelinating plaques, vasogenic edema, or infiltrative neoplasm.

VENTRICLES AND EXTRA-AXIAL SPACES:
The lateral ventricles, third ventricle, and fourth ventricle are symmetric and of normal caliber without hydrocephalus. The basal cisterns are patent. Sulcal prominence is mildly increased at the cerebral convexities, consistent with age-related cortical volume loss, within normal limits for this patient's age group. No extra-axial fluid collections. No subdural or epidural hematoma.

MIDLINE STRUCTURES:
The midline is not deviated. The falx cerebri and tentorium cerebelli are unremarkable in appearance. Pineal gland is normal; no pineal region mass.

PITUITARY GLAND AND SELLAR REGION:
The pituitary gland is normal in size and morphology. No sellar or suprasellar mass. The pituitary stalk is midline without deviation or thickening. No evidence of pituitary adenoma.

CARANIAL NERVES AND ORBITS (INCLUDED PORTIONS):
No abnormal enhancement along the course of the cranial nerves in the imaged portions. The optic nerves appear normal in caliber and signal bilaterally. Orbits are within normal limits to the extent included.

INTERNAL AUDITORY CANALS (INCLUDED PORTIONS):
The internal auditory canals appear symmetric and of normal caliber bilaterally. No enhancing lesion identified within the imaged portions of the internal auditory canals or at the cerebellopontine angles.

SKULL BASE AND CALVARIUM:
The skull base architecture is intact. No lytic or blastic osseous lesion is identified within the calvarium or skull base to suggest osseous metastatic disease in the imaged portions of bone. The visualized paranasal sinuses demonstrate mild mucosal thickening of the bilateral maxillary sinuses and ethmoid air cells, likely reflecting low-grade sinusitis or retention changes; this is incidental.

LEPTOMENINGES:
No leptomeningeal or pachymeningeal enhancement identified. No evidence of carcinomatous meningitis.

DURAL VENOUS SINUSES:
The superior sagittal sinus, transverse sinuses, sigmoid sinuses, and torcula are patent with normal flow void characteristics on conventional sequences. No evidence of dural venous sinus thrombosis.

CEREBELLUM AND BRAINSTEM:
The cerebellar hemispheres and vermis are symmetric without focal lesion. The pons, midbrain, and medulla are normal in morphology and signal. The fourth ventricle is in normal position without mass effect or obstruction. No tonsillar herniation; the cerebellar tonsils are positioned above the level of the foramen magnum.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. NO EVIDENCE OF INTRACRANIAL METASTATIC DISEASE. No parenchymal, leptomeningeal, or dural-based enhancing lesions identified on this gadolinium-enhanced examination.

2. Mild, scattered periventricular and subcortical white matter T2/FLAIR signal changes, non-enhancing, consistent with mild chronic microvascular ischemic changes appropriate for a patient of this age. No acute infarction or demyelinating pathology.

3. Mild age-appropriate cortical volume loss and scattered sinus mucosal changes as described; both are incidental findings.

4. Normal pituitary gland and sellar region without mass.

5. No hydrocephalus, midline shift, or extra-axial collection.

Overall, the brain MRI demonstrates no intracranial metastatic involvement. In the setting of newly diagnosed metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma with bilateral pulmonary metastases and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, this examination confirms that the central nervous system is not a site of apparent metastatic disease at this time.

COMMENT:
------------------------------------------------------------------------

The biopsy cores contain a biphasic neoplasm. The predominant component (approximately 80% of tumor area) shows classic clear cell RCC morphology: nests and alveoli of cells with abundant clear to finely granular cytoplasm, distinct cell membranes, and round to ovoid nuclei with prominent nucleoli visible at 10x magnification, consistent with WHO/ISUP nuclear grade 3. The intervening vasculature is delicate and arborizing, characteristic of clear cell RCC.

A sarcomatoid component is identified (approximately 20% of sampled area) and is composed of elongated spindle cells arranged in intersecting fascicles with markedly enlarged, pleomorphic nuclei, coarse chromatin, prominent nucleoli, and brisk mitotic activity (12 mitoses per 10 high-power fields in this component). Atypical mitoses are present. No rhabdoid cells (with eccentric nuclei and hyaline cytoplasmic inclusions) are identified in the material examined. Extensive geographic necrosis is present within both components.

The sarcomatoid morphology in the context of this renal primary is consistent with sarcomatoid differentiation of ccRCC (a recognized high-grade transformation), not a collision tumor or primary sarcoma. Immunohistochemistry supports this interpretation (see below).

Note: The degree of sarcomatoid differentiation estimated in this core biopsy (approximately 20%) may not be fully representative of the entire tumor given the inherently sampled nature of needle biopsy specimens.

                     termination (loss-of-function)
     Interpretation: PBRM1 encodes BAF180, the chromatin-targeting subunit of the
                     PBAF SWI/SNF chromatin-remodeling complex. PBRM1 is the second
                     most commonly mutated gene in ccRCC after VHL (mutated in
                     ~40% of cases). Loss of PBRM1 impairs transcriptional regulation
                     and DNA damage response, promoting tumor progression. Prior
                     immunohistochemistry on this specimen demonstrated loss of PBRM1
                     nuclear expression, consistent with this somatic mutation.
                     Emerging data suggest PBRM1-deficient ccRCC may have altered
                     sensitivity to immune checkpoint blockade. Retained BAP1 expression
                     by IHC (see pathology report) indicates a PBRM1-mutant / BAP1-
                     wild-type molecular subtype, historically associated with a
                     relatively more favorable prognosis within advanced ccRCC compared
                     to the BAP1-mutant subtype, though this patient has high-grade
                     sarcomatoid features.

  3. SETD2  p.L1916Rfs*15  (c.5745delC)                    PATHOGENIC — TIER II
     Variant type:   Frameshift deletion (1-bp deletion)
     Allele frequency: 31%
     Interpretation: SETD2 encodes a histone H3 lysine 36 trimethyltransferase
                     (H3K36me3), functioning as a tumor suppressor and key component
                     of the SWI/SNF chromatin-remodeling axis. SETD2 mutations occur
                     in approximately 10–15% of ccRCCs and are enriched in higher-grade
                     tumors. Loss of SETD2 function is associated with impaired
                     mismatch repair recruitment, microsatellite instability in a
                     subset of cases (not detected here), and altered epigenetic
                     regulation of oncogene expression. The subclonal allele frequency
                     (31%, lower than VHL at 48%) suggests this may be a secondary
                     evolutionary event acquired after VHL loss. SETD2 loss, together
                     with PBRM1 loss, defines a common epigenetically dysregulated
                     molecular subtype of ccRCC. No validated targeted therapy is
                     directly indicated by this finding beyond existing standard-of-care
                     options.

  4. KDM5C  p.T724M  (c.2171C>T)                           VUS — TIER III
     Variant type:   Missense
     Allele frequency: 18%
     Interpretation: KDM5C (also known as JARID1C) encodes a histone H3 lysine 4
                     demethylase located on chromosome Xp11.22. Recurrent somatic
                     mutations in KDM5C have been identified in ccRCC, although most
                     are classified as variants of uncertain significance pending
                     functional validation. The relatively low allele frequency (18%)
                     suggests a subclonal or possibly minor clone event. KDM5C loss
                     has been implicated in epigenetic dysregulation of gene expression
                     programs. No direct therapeutic target or clinical actionability
                     at this time. Monitored for reclassification as functional data
                     accumulate.
