COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS DETECTED:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  1. CDKN2A — Homozygous Deletion (High-Level Copy Number Loss)      PATHOGENIC
     Chromosomal region: 9p21.3
     Interpretation: CDKN2A encodes both p16INK4a (a CDK4/6 inhibitor promoting
                     G1/S cell cycle arrest) and p14ARF (a positive regulator of
                     p53 via MDM2 inhibition). Homozygous deletion results in complete
                     loss of both tumor suppressor functions. CDKN2A deletions are
                     found in approximately 15–20% of ccRCCs and are associated with
                     aggressive biology, higher Fuhrman/WHO-ISUP grade, and sarcomatoid
                     differentiation — the latter of which is prominently present in
                     this patient's tumor (22% sarcomatoid component confirmed on
                     nephrectomy specimen). This CNA is consistent with the high-grade
                     and sarcomatoid morphology observed histologically. CDK4/6
                     inhibitors (e.g., Ibrance, Kisqali) are not currently
                     standard-of-care in RCC and no clinical trials specifically
                     enrolling CDKN2A-deleted RCC are noted as immediately accessible;
                     however, this finding may inform future clinical trial eligibility.

  2. Chromosome 3p — Loss (Whole-Arm Loss of Heterozygosity)         PATHOGENIC
     Relevant genes at locus: VHL (3p25.3), PBRM1 (3p21.31), SETD2 (3p21.31),
                               BAP1 (3p21.31)
     Interpretation: Chromosome 3p loss is the hallmark chromosomal event in ccRCC,
                     occurring in >90% of cases. In conjunction with somatic VHL
                     p.R161* (above), this copy number loss establishes complete
                     biallelic VHL inactivation (mutation + LOH). The 3p deletion
                     also encompasses the PBRM1 and SETD2 loci, providing the second
                     hit for these tumor suppressors. This finding is entirely expected
                     and pathognomonic for ccRCC. The genomic LOH score (gLOH 18%;
                     see below) partially reflects this large-scale allelic imbalance.

  3. Chromosome 5q — Gain (Low-Level Amplification)                  LIKELY PATHOGENIC
     Interpretation: Chromosome 5q gain is one of the most common recurrent copy
                     number gains in ccRCC, occurring in approximately 60–70% of
                     cases. The relevant genes in this region include SQSTM1, NSD1,
                     and several genes involved in mTORC1 signaling regulation.
                     Chromosome 5q gain has been associated with better response to
                     VEGFR-targeted therapies in some retrospective analyses, though
                     prospective validation is lacking. No targeted therapy is directly
                     indicated by this finding.

THERAPEUTIC IMPLICATIONS:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  STANDARD-OF-CARE RELEVANCE:
  • VHL loss + 3p LOH: Confirms ccRCC diagnosis; supports VEGFR-targeted
    therapy sensitivity (suni, Votrient, cabozantinib, axitinib) and
    HIF-2α inhibitor (belzutifan) eligibility in future lines.
  • PBRM1 loss: Retrospective data (McDermott et al., NEJM 2018; Braun et al.,
    Nature Medicine 2019) suggest PBRM1-loss may be associated with differential
    response to Sutent vs. Tecentriq in combination regimens; PBRM1 loss
    has also been correlated with immune-inflamed tumor microenvironment features
    consistent with checkpoint inhibitor benefit.
  • High PD-L1 (CPS 55) + sarcomatoid differentiation: Strong rationale for
    first-line Yervoy + nivo (CheckMate 214 sarcomatoid subgroup
    demonstrated significant ORR and OS benefit).
  • CDKN2A homozygous deletion: Associated with aggressive biology and sarcomatoid
    features; does not alter standard first-line treatment selection at this time.

  CLINICAL TRIAL CONSIDERATIONS (INFORMATIONAL):
  • HIF-2α inhibitor (belzutifan): FDA-approved for VHL disease-associated RCC;
    emerging efficacy data in sporadic ccRCC with VHL loss (LITESPARK program).
    VHL p.R161* mutation makes this patient a potential candidate for belzutifan
    in later treatment lines if available.
  • PBRM1-stratified trials: Several ongoing trials use PBRM1 status for
    stratification or enrichment in immunotherapy combination studies.
  • CDKN2A deletion: May confer eligibility for CDK4/6 inhibitor trials in RCC
    (investigational).

METHODS NOTE:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  This report was generated using hybrid-capture next-generation sequencing of
  a 324-gene panel targeting clinically relevant cancer genes, including full
  exonic coverage of key tumor suppressors and oncogenes and select intronic
  regions for fusion/rearrangement detection. Somatic variants are reported at
  ≥5% allele frequency with ≥50x depth at the variant locus. Copy number
  alterations and structural variants are identified using bioinformatic
  algorithms applied to on-target and off-target read depth. MSI and TMB
  are calculated from sequencing data using validated computational methods.
  PD-L1 IHC was performed concurrently on FFPE sections using the 22C3
  pharmDx assay (Dako/Agilent), FDA-approved for PD-L1 CPS scoring in
  multiple tumor types. Germline analysis was performed on matched peripheral
  blood DNA with a focused hereditary cancer panel.

  This report has been reviewed and approved by a board-certified molecular
  pathologist and molecular genetic pathologist. Results should be interpreted
  in the clinical context by a qualified oncologist.

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Newly diagnosed metastatic right renal cell carcinoma, clear cell type, with sarcomatoid differentiation. Presented to the Genitourinary Oncology Multidisciplinary Tumor Board for case review and treatment planning.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman of West African descent, a retired secondary school administrator, who presented to her primary care physician approximately 6 weeks ago with several months of right flank discomfort, fatigue, and unintentional weight loss of approximately 8 lbs over 3 months. Initial workup revealed microscopic hematuria on urinalysis. She was referred for cross-sectional imaging.

CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast identified a large heterogeneous right renal mass measuring 11.4 cm with central necrosis and imaging-confirmed involvement of the right renal vein extending into the infrarenal inferior vena cava, with an IVC thrombus estimated at approximately 3 cm in length (non-occlusive). Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy was present, with the largest right para-aortic node measuring 2.8 cm, an aortocaval node of 2.2 cm, and a right common iliac chain node of 1.5 cm. Bilateral pulmonary nodules were identified, consistent with pulmonary metastases: the dominant right lower lobe lesion measuring 1.9 cm, right upper lobe 1.1 cm, left lower lobe 0.8 cm, and left upper lobe 0.6 cm. No hepatic, osseous, or adrenal metastases were identified on initial staging. A dedicated MRI of the brain with and without contrast was performed as part of staging and was negative for intracranial metastases.

A CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right renal mass was performed for histologic confirmation prior to MDT presentation. Pathology confirmed renal cell carcinoma, clear cell type, WHO-ISUP grade 3, with sarcomatoid differentiation identified in approximately 20% of the sampled specimen. There was no rhabdoid differentiation. Immunohistochemistry was strongly supportive: PAX8 positive, CA9 (carbonic anhydrase IX) diffusely positive membranous pattern, CD10 positive, vimentin positive in the sarcomatoid component. PD-L1 IHC using the 22C3 antibody showed a tumor proportion score (TPS) of 40% and a combined positive score (CPS) estimated at 55, indicating high PD-L1 expression. PBRM1 expression was lost by IHC, consistent with a somatic loss-of-function alteration. Ki-67 proliferation index was elevated, particularly in the sarcomatoid component (approximately 35%).

Comprehensive genomic profiling (324-gene panel) was performed on the biopsy specimen. Key findings included: VHL p.R161* (pathogenic nonsense mutation, allele frequency 48%), PBRM1 splice site mutation c.2977+1G>T (pathogenic, allele frequency 42%), SETD2 frameshift p.L1916Rfs*15 (pathogenic, allele frequency 31%), KDM5C p.T724M (variant of uncertain significance), and CDKN2A homozygous deletion. Chromosome 3p loss was noted, consistent with VHL locus LOH. Tumor mutational burden was 6.2 mutations/megabase (intermediate-low). Microsatellite status: stable (MSS), mismatch repair proficient (pMMR). Germline analysis revealed no pathogenic variants in VHL, SDHA/B/C/D, FH, TSC1/2, MET, or FLCN — no heritable renal cancer syndrome identified.

The case was formally staged as Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition; cT3bN2M1) clear cell renal cell carcinoma. The patient has been assigned to the IMDC intermediate-risk group (2 adverse factors: Karnofsky performance status below 80 at a nadir during the workup period, and hemoglobin below the lower limit of normal on presentation, measured at 10.4 g/dL at the time of initial laboratory evaluation).

The patient was referred to our Genitourinary Oncology program and her case is being presented to the multidisciplinary tumor board today for formal recommendations regarding cytoreductive surgery and systemic therapy sequencing.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman with newly diagnosed Stage IV (cT3bN2M1) clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation (20%), presenting with a large primary right renal mass with IVC thrombus, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and bilateral pulmonary metastases. IMDC intermediate-risk (2 factors). PD-L1 high (CPS 55). MSS/pMMR. TMB intermediate-low. No germline hereditary renal cancer syndrome identified.

The following recommendations were reached by consensus of the Genitourinary MDT:

1. CYTOREDUCTIVE NEPHRECTOMY — RECOMMENDED
Rationale: The patient has ECOG PS 1, IMDC intermediate risk, and the vast majority of her tumor burden is in the primary right renal mass (11.4 cm) and regional retroperitoneal lymph nodes. The pulmonary metastases are relatively small (largest 1.9 cm). The IVC thrombus is level I-II and technically resectable. Contemporary data (including the CARMENA trial context) suggests that cytoreductive nephrectomy (CN) may benefit selected patients with IMDC intermediate-risk disease, particularly when — as in this case — the primary accounts for a dominant proportion of disease burden, retroperitoneal lymph node clearance may improve local disease control, and sarcomatoid histology in the primary creates particular urgency for reducing tumor volume prior to immunotherapy.

The urologic surgery team has reviewed the imaging and confirms that the planned surgical approach is: open midline laparotomy, right radical nephrectomy, retroperitoneal lymph node dissection, and level I-II IVC thrombectomy. No cardiopulmonary bypass is anticipated. The renal vein and IVC wall margins are expected to be achieved. Adrenal gland preservation on the contralateral side is not applicable; right adrenal will be assessed intraoperatively.

Surgical risk counseling to be completed by urologic surgery. Patient has been informed of the rationale for cytoreduction in the metastatic setting — that this is not curative but aims to reduce tumor burden, potentially improve immunotherapy response, and address local symptoms (flank discomfort, hematuria risk, IVC-related concerns).

2. SYSTEMIC THERAPY — POST-OPERATIVE FIRST-LINE: Yervoy + Opdivo
Rationale: Following recovery from cytoreductive nephrectomy (approximately 6-8 weeks post-op), the patient will be initiated on dual checkpoint inhibition with ipi (Yervoy) 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks x4 doses in combination with nivo (Opdivo) 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks x4 induction doses, followed by Opdivo 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance monotherapy. This regimen is supported by the CheckMate 214 trial, which demonstrated improved overall survival for ipi/nivo versus suni in IMDC intermediate- and poor-risk patients. Of particular relevance, retrospective and exploratory analyses from CheckMate 214 have shown a particularly pronounced benefit for patients with sarcomatoid differentiation — the sarcomatoid subgroup analysis showed markedly higher objective response rates and improved survival with ipi/nivo, including complete responses, compared with VEGFR-targeted monotherapy. The patient's high PD-L1 expression (CPS 55) further supports an immunotherapy-based approach. CDKN2A deletion and the overall genomic profile do not identify any approved targeted therapy alteration that would change the recommended first-line approach.

The alternative of Lenvima plus pembro (CLEAR trial) or cabozantinib plus Opdivo (CheckMate 9ER) was discussed; however, given the sarcomatoid subgroup data favoring dual checkpoint blockade with ipi/nivo and the high PD-L1 status, the MDT consensus was in favor of the CheckMate 214 regimen. Axitinib/pembro (KEYNOTE-426) was also considered.

Pre-treatment baseline labs (CBC, CMP, TSH, AM cortisol, LFTs, lipase, urinalysis) will be obtained prior to cycle 1 initiation. Standard acetaminophen and diphenhydramine premedication per institutional protocol. Patient will be educated regarding immune-related adverse events (irAEs): colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies (thyroid, adrenal, pituitary), nephritis, skin reactions.

3. STAGING AND RESPONSE MONITORING
Baseline restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis approximately 6 weeks post-operatively (prior to initiating systemic therapy) to establish a new post-nephrectomy baseline for response assessment. Subsequent restaging CT every 12 weeks during systemic therapy. MRI brain at baseline post-op (then as clinically indicated). Bone scan not indicated at this time unless symptoms develop.

4. SUPPORTIVE CARE
- Anemia: Hgb 10.4 g/dL — likely anemia of inflammation and/or chronic disease in the context of advanced malignancy and mildly elevated LDH. Iron studies to be checked. Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents deferred given active malignancy context; transfusion threshold at Hgb <8 g/dL or per symptom burden.
- Hypertension: Continue amlodipine 10 mg/day. Monitor blood pressure closely during systemic therapy, particularly if VEGFR-targeted therapy is eventually needed.
- Lipid management: Continue atorvastatin.
- Bone health: DEXA scan to assess bone density (baseline); calcium and vitamin D supplementation recommended.
- Nutrition: Dietitian referral given weight loss and anticipated surgical recovery.
- Palliative care: Early palliative care integration discussed with patient and family given Stage IV diagnosis. Patient expressed strong desire to pursue all available treatment options with the primary goals of maximizing duration and quality of life and remaining present and functional for her family.

5. GENETIC COUNSELING
Germline analysis via the CGP platform did not identify pathogenic variants in VHL, SDHA/B/C/D, FH, TSC1/2, MET, or FLCN. No referral to genetic counseling required at this time, as no heritable syndrome is identified. Family history is not concerning for hereditary RCC. Finding communicated to patient.

6. CLINICAL TRIAL CONSIDERATION
No currently enrolling institutional trials for this exact patient profile (metastatic ccRCC with sarcomatoid features, intermediate risk, newly diagnosed) at this time. Patient consented to be notified if a relevant trial opens.

Return to GU oncology clinic for pre-treatment evaluation prior to systemic therapy initiation (approximately 6-8 weeks post-nephrectomy). Referral placed to urologic surgery for pre-operative counseling and surgical scheduling.

Case reviewed and discussed by multidisciplinary team. Documentation completed by GU Medical Oncology attending in collaboration with the MDT.

GROSS DESCRIPTION

Specimen A: Received fresh, labeled "right kidney," is a right radical nephrectomy specimen consisting of the kidney with attached perinephric/periureteral adipose tissue. The specimen measures 16.5 x 11.8 x 9.0 cm and weighs 764 grams (prior to sectioning and lymph node dissection). The external surface is covered by Gerota's fat, which has been extensively sampled. The renal hilum contains the main renal vein, renal artery, and proximal ureter. The renal vein is dilated and firm; on sectioning, it contains visible tumor extending to the cut end (renal vein stump). The ureter measures 3.5 cm in length and 0.4 cm in diameter; the ureteral margin is serially sectioned.

Serial sectioning of the kidney reveals a large, poorly circumscribed mass centered in the mid-to-upper pole of the right kidney measuring 11.8 x 10.3 x 9.5 cm. The mass is predominantly solid with extensive central necrosis estimated at greater than 50% of the tumor volume; the necrotic areas appear yellow-tan with areas of hemorrhage and cystic degeneration. The viable peripheral tumor is tan-white to yellow with a glistening, gelatinous quality in regions, admixed with firm, grayish-white areas (corresponding to sarcomatoid component). The mass infiltrates the renal sinus fat along a broad front; the closest inked margin at the renal sinus is approximately 2 mm and is serially submitted. The tumor does not breach the renal capsule in the sampled sections (renal capsule intact). The uninvolved kidney parenchyma is unremarkable; the non-neoplastic kidney is compressed but shows no significant nephrosclerosis. Representative sections of the non-neoplastic kidney are submitted.

Specimen B: Received fresh, labeled "IVC thrombus," consists of an irregular, friable, dark-red to tan-white thrombus measuring 4.2 x 1.5 x 1.3 cm in aggregate. The specimen has areas of firm, tan-white viable tumor admixed with organizing thrombus. A segment of the IVC wall is attached (1.8 x 1.5 cm); the external surface of the IVC wall is inked and serially sectioned perpendicular to the wall; no gross tumor invades the IVC wall. Representative sections submitted in toto.

Specimens C through P (Retroperitoneal Lymph Nodes, by station): Multiple separately submitted lymph node packets are received, labeled by anatomic station (right para-aortic, aortocaval, right common iliac, right paracaval, and others per surgical labeling). Total of 14 lymph nodes identified after fat clearing and serial sectioning. Lymph nodes range from 0.3 to 3.1 cm in greatest dimension. Nine lymph nodes are grossly enlarged and exhibit firm, tan-white to yellow, solid cut surfaces consistent with nodal replacement by metastatic tumor; the largest (3.1 cm) extends beyond the nodal capsule into perinodal fat (extranodal extension). Five lymph nodes are grossly unremarkable (0.3-0.7 cm, soft, tan). All lymph nodes are submitted in their entirety or representatively for large nodes, with perinodal fat.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION

Specimen A (Right Kidney, Radical Nephrectomy):
The renal mass consists of a biphasic tumor. The predominant component (approximately 78% of viable tumor) demonstrates classic clear cell renal cell carcinoma histology: sheets, nests, and acini of tumor cells with abundant optically clear cytoplasm (due to glycogen and lipid content, dissolved in processing), well-defined cell membranes, and variably prominent nucleoli. The vascularity is characteristically rich, with numerous delicate sinusoidal blood vessels. WHO/ISUP nuclear grade in the conventional clear cell component is grade 3, with prominent macronucleoli visible at 10x objective in the high-grade areas, and occasional nuclear pleomorphism.

The sarcomatoid component comprises approximately 22% of viable tumor volume and is identified in multiple areas including the renal sinus interface and intravascular extensions. This component consists of fascicles and sheets of elongated, spindle-shaped cells with markedly pleomorphic nuclei, coarse chromatin, prominent nucleoli, and frequent atypical mitotic figures (up to 9 mitoses per 10 high-power fields in the most active areas). Zones of transition between conventional ccRCC and sarcomatoid morphology are identified in several sections, supporting dedifferentiation from the ccRCC component. No rhabdoid differentiation is identified (no eccentric nuclei with hyaline cytoplasmic inclusions and prominent nucleoli characteristic of rhabdoid morphology).

Tumor necrosis is extensive, occupying an estimated >50% of total tumor volume; necrosis is of the coagulative type with ghost outlines of necrotic cells.

Lymphovascular invasion is identified in multiple sections at the tumor-sinus interface, consisting of tumor cell clusters and sarcomatoid emboli within thin-walled vascular spaces in the renal sinus fat. Perineural invasion is not identified.

The renal vein contains tumor extending to the vein margin (renal vein stump margin); the tumor in the renal vein is viable and includes both conventional and sarcomatoid components. The renal capsule is intact without definitive capsular breach in sections examined; the perinephric fat is not involved by tumor. Renal sinus fat is involved by direct tumor extension; the inked renal sinus margin is clear by 2 mm at the closest point.

The ureteral margin is negative for carcinoma.

Non-neoplastic renal parenchyma shows mild global glomerulosclerosis (approximately 12%), mild tubular atrophy, and mild interstitial fibrosis, consistent with age-related nephrosclerosis. No other significant nephropathology.

Specimen B (IVC Thrombus):
Histologic sections show viable tumor within organizing and acute thrombus. The viable tumor component comprises both conventional clear cell carcinoma (approximately 85% of viable tumor) and sarcomatoid morphology (approximately 15%). The IVC wall is present in sections and shows no definitive transmural invasion by tumor; the adventitial surface is free of tumor. Tumor thrombus is adherent to but not invasive through the IVC wall in sections examined.

Specimens C-P (Retroperitoneal Lymph Nodes, 14 Nodes, 9 Positive):
Nine of fourteen lymph nodes show replacement by metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma. The metastatic deposits contain both conventional clear cell and sarcomatoid components. Extranodal extension is identified in the largest node (3.1 cm), with sarcomatoid tumor cells extending into perinodal adipose tissue. Five lymph nodes are negative for malignancy and show reactive lymphoid hyperplasia.

COMMENT

This cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy specimen demonstrates high-grade (WHO/ISUP grade 4) clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid dedifferentiation (22%), extensive necrosis, and lymphovascular invasion. Sarcomatoid differentiation is associated with a significantly worse prognosis and is considered a grade 4 designation by definition under WHO/ISUP grading criteria. The finding of sarcomatoid features in both the primary tumor and multiple lymph node metastases is consistent with the biopsy results and underscores the aggressive biology.

Extranodal extension in the largest metastatic lymph node (3.1 cm) and the extent of nodal burden (9/14 nodes positive, pN2) are adverse prognostic factors. The IVC wall is histologically uninvolved, which is surgically favorable (Level I-II thrombus without IVC wall invasion).

PBRM1 loss of expression by IHC is confirmed and correlates with the known splice site mutation on comprehensive genomic profiling. BAP1 retention is noted; concurrent PBRM1 loss without BAP1 loss may carry distinct prognostic implications (PBRM1-loss, BAP1-intact subtype in some classification frameworks).

The specimen has been reviewed at multidisciplinary genitourinary tumor board. Correlation with clinical, radiologic, and molecular findings is recommended.

PD-L1 IHC (22C3) results on the nephrectomy specimen are PENDING and will be reported in a subsequent addendum.

CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old female with Stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma, sarcomatoid features, status post right radical cytoreductive nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection and inferior vena cava thrombectomy approximately six weeks prior. Presenting for pre-treatment baseline imaging prior to initiation of systemic immunotherapy. History of bilateral pulmonary metastases on staging imaging. Evaluate for interval change and establish new baseline.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed in the portal venous phase following the administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast. Oral contrast was not administered. Images were reconstructed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes at 2.5 mm slice thickness. Lung windows, soft tissue windows, and bone windows were reviewed. Comparison is made to the staging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed approximately two months prior to this examination.


FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The central airways are patent. The trachea is midline. There is no endobronchial lesion.

Bilateral pulmonary metastases are again identified and appear minimally increased in size compared to the prior staging CT, though the degree of change is modest and within the range of measurement variability as well as interval growth given the approximately 8-week interval. The dominant right lower lobe lesion, located in the posterior basal segment, now measures 2.1 cm in greatest transaxial dimension (previously 1.9 cm on staging CT). This lesion is well-circumscribed with a smooth outer margin and is surrounded by normal aerated lung parenchyma. There is no associated pleural involvement or chest wall contact. A second right-sided lesion in the posterior segment of the right upper lobe measures 1.2 cm (previously 1.1 cm). In the left lung, a nodule in the posterior basal segment of the left lower lobe measures 0.9 cm (previously 0.8 cm). A left upper lobe lesion in the apicoposterior segment measures 0.7 cm (previously 0.6 cm). All four lesions demonstrate soft tissue attenuation without internal calcification or cavitation. There is no evidence of new pulmonary nodularity or additional sites of parenchymal disease beyond the four previously documented lesions.

No pulmonary consolidation or ground-glass opacity to suggest infection or aspiration.

No pleural effusion bilaterally.

No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinum is not widened. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes are identified. The largest mediastinal node measures approximately 0.8 cm in short axis (within normal limits). No suspicious mediastinal mass.

Heart and Pericardium: The cardiac silhouette is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta demonstrates no aneurysmal dilatation or dissection. Mild atherosclerotic calcification of the aortic arch.

Chest Wall and Bony Thorax: No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions within the ribs, thoracic vertebrae, sternum, or clavicles. No pathologic fracture. Mild degenerative changes noted at several thoracic disc levels.


ABDOMEN:

Right Nephrectomy Bed and Retroperitoneum: There is expected post-surgical change in the right retroperitoneum consistent with the documented history of right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection and IVC thrombectomy. The right renal fossa demonstrates a seroma/hematoma cavity measuring approximately 7.4 x 5.2 cm (craniocaudal x transaxial dimension) with heterogeneous fluid-attenuation contents, consistent with evolving post-operative seroma and hemostatic debris. The cavity walls are thin and regular without abnormal internal nodularity or enhancement to suggest residual or recurrent tumor in the operative bed. Surgical clips are seen throughout the right retroperitoneum at sites of prior lymph node dissection. Small amounts of residual surgical staple artifact are present along the right lateral retroperitoneum. There is no definite residual retroperitoneal adenopathy; multiple small foci of soft tissue attenuation are present in the aortocaval region, measuring up to 0.8 cm in short axis, which appear consistent with post-surgical fibrotic change or residual small nodes following nodal dissection, but are not confidently measurable as pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in this post-operative setting. No new retroperitoneal adenopathy identified.

Inferior Vena Cava: The IVC is patent throughout its visualized course with no evidence of residual intraluminal thrombus. The IVC caliber is normal. The thrombectomy site demonstrates expected post-operative change without focal narrowing or hemodynamically significant stenosis.

Left Kidney: The left kidney is normally positioned with normal parenchymal thickness and enhancement. There is no hydronephrosis. No renal mass lesion. A small 4 mm left renal cortical cyst is noted, unchanged from prior.

Liver: The liver is normal in size and morphology. The parenchyma demonstrates homogeneous enhancement without focal lesion. No hepatic metastasis identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. The portal and hepatic veins are patent.

Gallbladder and Biliary: The gallbladder is present and demonstrates no wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, or gallstones. The common bile duct is not dilated.

Spleen: Normal in size and homogeneous enhancement. No splenic lesion.

Pancreas: The pancreas enhances uniformly without focal mass or ductal dilatation. The pancreatic duct is not dilated.

Adrenal Glands: Both adrenal glands appear normal without focal nodule or mass. No adrenal metastasis identified on this examination.

Gastrointestinal Tract: The stomach, small bowel, and visualized colon are unremarkable without wall thickening or obstruction. No free intraperitoneal air. Mild prominence of pericolonic fat stranding is present in the sigmoid region, likely incidental/age-related change.

Peritoneum and Mesentery: No ascites. No peritoneal implants or omental nodularity to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis.

Abdominal Aorta: The abdominal aorta demonstrates no aneurysmal change. Mild calcific atherosclerotic plaque is noted along the infrarenal aorta, unchanged.


PELVIS:

Lymph Nodes: No pathologically enlarged pelvic or inguinal lymph nodes. Previously documented right common iliac chain node is no longer identifiable following lymph node dissection, consistent with surgical removal.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is decompressed but appears grossly intact without wall thickening or intraluminal filling defect.

Female Pelvis: The uterus and bilateral adnexa are not separately characterized on this examination due to post-menopausal atrophy; no pelvic mass is identified. No free pelvic fluid.

Musculoskeletal: No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions within the pelvic bones, sacrum, or proximal femora. Mild degenerative changes are noted at the bilateral sacroiliac joints.


IMPRESSION:

1. BILATERAL PULMONARY METASTASES, FOUR LESIONS, MINIMALLY INCREASED COMPARED TO STAGING SCAN: All four previously identified bilateral pulmonary metastatic lesions are again seen and demonstrate very modest interval growth over approximately two months since the staging examination. The dominant lesion in the right lower lobe now measures 2.1 cm (previously 1.9 cm). The right upper lobe lesion measures 1.2 cm (previously 1.1 cm). The left lower lobe lesion measures 0.9 cm (previously 0.8 cm), and the left upper lobe lesion measures 0.7 cm (previously 0.6 cm). No additional pulmonary lesions are identified. These four lesions represent the target lesions for systemic therapy response assessment going forward.

2. POST-SURGICAL CHANGES IN THE RIGHT RETROPERITONEUM: Expected post-nephrectomy changes with evolving right renal fossa seroma/hematoma. No imaging evidence of residual viable tumor in the nephrectomy bed. The IVC is now patent without residual intraluminal thrombus following thrombectomy. Post-lymph node dissection changes in the aortocaval region without convincing pathological residual adenopathy.

3. NO NEW SITES OF METASTATIC DISEASE: No hepatic, adrenal, osseous, or peritoneal metastatic deposits. No new retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Brain not imaged on this examination.

4. LEFT KIDNEY UNREMARKABLE: The solitary remaining left kidney demonstrates normal enhancement and morphology without hydronephrosis or parenchymal lesion.

This study is submitted as the new pre-treatment baseline for systemic therapy response assessment. Clinical correlation is recommended.
